sections.title: Summary
sections.raw_text: The investigation into the assassination of Martin Luther King was comprehensive and thorough [1, pp.17-18]. A task force was established to review the FBI's investigation, examining the character and completeness of the murder investigation [1, pp.17-18]. The review team examined files from various FBI field offices, including Memphis, Atlanta, and Los Angeles, and scanned pertinent newspaper clippings [5, pp.19-21]. The task force reviewed over 200,000 entries, many with multiple pages [5, pp.19-21]. Excisions were made to protect privacy rights, sensitive FBI sources and methods, and classified material, and changes were made to correct factual errors [2, pp.5-7], [3, pp.4-6]. No decision has been made on whether the report will be released to the public [2, pp.5-7], [3, pp.4-6]. The Department has not closed the Martin Luther King file, and numerous allegations of possible involvement of co-conspirators are being investigated [4, pp.14-15]. As of the current point in the review, nothing has been found to give a different emphasis or new clues to the question of involvement in the crime [4, pp.14-15]. There is no evidence of electronic surveillance of Dr. King in Memphis, but FBI agents did observe sanitation worker's strike activities for intelligence purposes [6, pp.32-34].

sections.title: Potential Risks
sections.raw_text: Potential risks to the firm may arise from the systematic program of harassment [mkl1a, pp.15-16], which could lead to specific actions being taken against the firm. The evidence suggests that a review of certain materials may be necessary to determine which ones could be legally destroyed [mkl1a, pp.13-14]. Additionally, the possibility of criminal redress being time-barred, and civil remedies being available but potentially not beneficial, may pose risks to the firm [mkl1a, pp.13-14]. However, no clear evidence is found regarding the potential consequences of releasing the report to the public [mkl1a, pp.5-7], as no decision has been made on whether the report will be released. The firm should also consider the potential risks associated with the protection of sensitive FBI sources and methods, as well as the deletion of classified material [mkl1a, pp.5-7].

sections.title: Missing Information
sections.raw_text: Several key pieces of information are missing from the available evidence. The decision on whether the report will be released to the public has not been made, as stated in [1, pp.5-7] and [2, pp.4-6]. Additionally, the reasons behind the detailing of Wallace and Newsun to Fire Station No. 33 are unclear, with contradictory statements from various individuals [3, pp.43-44] and [4, pp.45-47]. The FBI's files and records have been examined, but the extent of the examination is not fully disclosed, with over 200,000 entries reviewed [5, pp.19-21]. Furthermore, the motivations behind the detailing of Wallace and Newsun, who were supporters of the sanitation workers strike, are not explicitly stated [6, pp.42-43]. No clear evidence is found regarding the ultimate outcome of the report or the official explanation for the detailing of these individuals.

sections.title: Supporting Evidence
sections.raw_text:
The investigation into the murder of Martin Luther King Jr. involved a thorough review of various files and records [1, pp.19-21]. The FBI examined files from multiple field offices, including those in Memphis, Atlanta, and Los Angeles, and xeroxed pertinent documents for review in Washington [1, pp.19-21]. The Bureau also scanned newspaper clipping files maintained by the Department and its field offices [1, pp.19-21]. In terms of papers examined, more than 200,000 entries were reviewed [1, pp.19-21]. 

The investigative emphasis shifted from following James Earl Ray's trail to identifying him, with the Bureau initiating a comparative search of latent fingerprints believed to be Ray's [2, pp.67-69]. This effort eventually paid off when a fingerprint comparison was made with James Earl Ray's fingerprint card [2, pp.67-69]. 

The task force reviewed various documents, including a map showing the location of tactical units, a diagram of the crime scene, and an autopsy report [3, pp.8-10]. The team also conducted over 30 interviews, which were helpful in supplementing the results of interviews done during the murder investigation [6, pp.22-23]. 

The task force is satisfied that the FBI did a credible and thorough job in attempting to identify any possible conspiracy or persons involved in the murder [4, pp.72-73]. The Bureau immediately interviewed sources of allegations and attempted to identify and interview alleged participants [4, pp.72-73]. However, the evidence does not provide a comprehensive list of all the files and records reviewed or all the interviews conducted. No clear evidence was found regarding the specific conclusions drawn from the reviewed files and records, beyond the satisfaction with the FBI's efforts [4, pp.72-73].
